269158.explanation: Dog tax is not an end in itself. We choose for a fairer distribution of taxes.

269159.explanation: Refugees are ordinary residents with the right to safe housing. Smallingerland applies an urgency regulation and follows national policy so they can fairly and lawfully receive priority for social rental housing.

269160.explanation: We believe integration is important and want newcomers to the Netherlands (such as refugees, asylum seekers, and status holders) to participate as quickly and well as possible in society. This includes accelerated access to work, language education, and social participation.

269162.explanation: We believe everyone should be able to participate and are happy to advise entrepreneurs on this. In public buildings, the municipality contributes financially.

269164.explanation: Sports clubs are the heart of our society and we must support them at all times. We choose sport as a basic provision, accessible to everyone.

269166.explanation: The neighborhood police officer is the face of the community and indispensable for trust and safety. We invest in proximity with extra neighborhood or youth workers, and we also advocate for more neighborhood officers at the police.

269167.explanation: We do not see camera surveillance as a (standard) solution but believe it can be deployed temporarily and targeted for serious nuisance, with respect for privacy and in combination with other measures and consultation with city center/hospitality entrepreneurs.

269168.explanation: We want everyone to be able to travel safely, which means obeying rules and acting against dangerous behavior, regardless of the vehicle.

269170.explanation: We believe that at least 35% of all newly built homes must be social rental. Currently, 31% of homes in the municipality are social housing. By increasing this share, we ensure affordable housing remains available to those who need it most.

269173.explanation: Too many people currently fall just outside the regulations while desperately needing help. By raising the income threshold, we ensure more residents receive support and can participate in Smallingerland.

269174.explanation: Social assistance is a safety net, not a final destination. We want to offer people perspective by investing in education, guidance, and social work. This way, we help people move forward, strengthen their independence, and ensure everyone can participate.

269175.explanation: Long-term unemployment requires customized solutions. We want more 'partially' subsidized jobs so people gain work experience, get structure, and regain perspective. With good guidance, we help people sustainably return to paid work.

269176.explanation: Payment security is a right; those who cannot pay should not be pushed further into problems by municipal taxes.

269177.explanation: It depends on the situation. Education increases the chance of sustainable employment. We believe people on social assistance pursuing education or retraining should be temporarily exempt from job application requirements, giving them real space to invest in their future.

269178.explanation: We believe informal caregivers on social assistance should have the space to fulfill their caregiving duties well. Therefore, people who care for a family member or acquaintance should be temporarily exempt from job-seeking obligations, allowing them to provide support without pressure from job application requirements.

269179.explanation: Parking in the neighborhoods should be possible; during the redesign of the neighborhoods, there should be a better focus on less car use and more greenery.

269181.explanation: Parking must be arranged smartly, ensuring sufficient accessibility, but also space for bicycles, greenery, and social meeting places.

269182.explanation: We believe transport is essential for livable villages and village connections. Therefore, the municipality should invest in tailored transport, such as demand-responsive buses, so that everyone, including the elderly and people without cars, can participate independently in society.

269183.explanation: We want to make the center of Drachten more livable and safer by introducing more car-free streets. This creates more space for pedestrians, cyclists, and social encounters, making the center more attractive to visitors and shops.

269184.explanation: This increases traffic safety, protects pedestrians and vulnerable road users such as children and the elderly, and makes streets more livable and pleasant to live in.

269185.explanation: We believe everyone has the right to care that suits them. People should always be able to choose a care provider that fits, so that care is personal, respectful, and appropriate for everyone.

269186.explanation: We believe each village and neighborhood should have its own social and cultural community center. This creates places for meeting, activities, and connection, allowing residents to get to know each other better and thereby strengthening social cohesion in the municipality.

269187.explanation: We believe that quality should always come before price in youth care. The municipality must select organizations based on expertise, effectiveness, and the well-being of children and young people, so they receive the support they truly need.

269188.explanation: We believe the municipality may accommodate more asylum seekers than strictly required if necessary, provided this is done carefully, good consultations take place, and there is sufficient guidance, housing, and social support.

269189.explanation: We believe EVERYONE must live safely, including at asylum seeker reception locations. The municipality must address nuisance EVERYWHERE carefully and effectively, protecting local residents without hindering the reception or support for asylum seekers.

269190.explanation: We believe asylum seekers should receive good opportunities to integrate from the moment they arrive. Therefore, they should be given mandatory language lessons and practical guidance so they can participate independently in society and more quickly find their way in work, school, and neighborhood.

269191.explanation: We believe everyone in the municipality should have access to information. Therefore, letters, forms, and online messages should be available in both Frisian and Dutch, so residents can fully participate and receive clear communication.

269192.explanation: We believe village and neighborhood festivals should remain accessible. Therefore, the municipality should reduce permit costs, so more residents can participate, communities are strengthened, and local social cohesion grows.

269193.explanation: We believe sports should be accessible to every child. The municipality must ensure children can play sports at minimal cost. As far as we are concerned, free until age 18, so all children can participate, grow up healthy, and develop social skills.

269194.explanation: We believe sports should be accessible to everyone, including people with low incomes. The municipality must continue to provide support so everyone can play sports, stay healthy, and participate in society.

269195.explanation: We believe students should gain practical experience. The municipality should collaborate with local businesses to create more internships and apprenticeships for vocational students, so they develop skills, gain work experience, and improve their job market opportunities.

269196.explanation: We want to expand nature and meadow bird areas, but always in consultation with farmers and stakeholders. Expansion may affect agricultural land but will be done carefully to keep a balance between nature, agriculture, and the living environment.

269197.explanation: We believe residents should have more influence over their environment. Therefore, the municipality should allocate a larger part of the budget to neighborhood and village projects, strengthening local initiatives and social cohesion.

269198.explanation: We believe young people need their own places to meet, engage in activities, and develop themselves. Therefore, every neighborhood and village should have its own youth meeting place, so they feel safe and involved in their own environment.

269200.explanation: We believe welfare benefits are a safety net. Reducing benefits for not meeting obligations harms people who need help. The municipality should provide support and guidance for work and education so people gain perspective and can progress.

269201.explanation: We believe the municipality should help residents with debts through counseling, budget advice, and prevention. Taking over debts is not a structural solution; support should empower people and help them sustainably improve their financial situation.

269202.explanation: We see Drachten-South as a potential location for housing development. The municipality must take control and ensure sufficient affordable housing, paying attention to landscape, water, and quality of life. Only in this way can we build responsibly for the future.

269204.explanation: We find good accessibility important for Northern Netherlands. Lobbying for a rail connection can contribute to better connections, economic opportunities, and accessibility. Careful consideration must always be given to benefits, costs, and impact on the environment and nature.

269207.explanation: We believe young people need safe and accessible places to meet. By investing more in meeting spaces for youth, we strengthen their development, prevent nuisance, and increase engagement with the neighborhood.

269208.explanation: We believe camera surveillance should only be used temporarily and for specific purposes. It is a serious measure that must be regularly evaluated and ended as soon as the goal is achieved, respecting residents' privacy and freedom.

269209.explanation: We believe property tax (OZB) can be raised to invest in livable neighborhoods, sports, and facilities. Those who can financially contribute should pay a little more so that children and residents with low incomes can participate. This way the strongest shoulders carry the heaviest burden.

269210.explanation: We believe entrepreneurs should have space to expand their terraces. This stimulates the local economy, creates lively streets, and offers residents and visitors cozy places to meet.

269211.explanation: We believe multi-year subsidies provide certainty for organizations and projects. This allows initiatives to plan long-term, perform better, and have a sustainable impact in the municipality.

269212.explanation: We believe permit applications should be processed quickly and efficiently. By investing in this, the municipality helps residents and entrepreneurs faster, prevents delays, and ensures projects can start smoothly and well.

269158.explanation: The CDA believes that the dog tax is only fair and well-explained if there is a direct return service. Currently, this is not the case.

269159.explanation: The CDA believes everyone should be treated equally. Priority for one group increases pressure on social housing and feelings of inequality. The CDA chooses to accelerate building more social housing so housing seekers gain perspective without priority systems.

269160.explanation: The CDA considers integration important but sees it as a national responsibility. The municipality supports where needed, but the main care and funding for newcomers lie with the national government, so local funds primarily go to facilities for all residents.

269162.explanation: The CDA is positive about accessibility but believes the needed measures should be considered on a case-by-case basis. We do not rule out support and work together with entrepreneurs to enable small or large adaptations.

269164.explanation: The CDA fully supports our associations and communities. Where possible, we advocate exemption from municipal taxes and levies and offer full support so they can continue to exist and connect people in Smallingerland.

269166.explanation: The CDA values the efforts of the police and neighborhood officers. We are positive about consultation hours but will assess per neighborhood and village what is practically feasible to effectively support residents and keep them involved.

269167.explanation: The CDA considers a safe nightlife area important. We support camera surveillance in Drachten where necessary to prevent nuisance and crime and allow visitors to safely enjoy the nightlife area.

269168.explanation: The CDA stands for more control and a firm approach to antisocial behavior. We want fatbikers and other troublemakers to be firmly tackled so roads, paths, and public spaces remain safe for everyone.

269170.explanation: The CDA believes that 30% of all homes should be social rental housing so everyone can find a suitable home. This aligns with the national standard. This ensures affordable housing and keeps the housing market in Smallingerland moving for all residents.

269173.explanation: The CDA wants aid to reach people who truly need it. By raising the income limit, more people can receive support. This prevents debts and strengthens the poverty pact. Everyone should be able to participate.

269174.explanation: The CDA values participation. Those who can should contribute according to their ability through socially meaningful work. This increases opportunities for employment and involvement, of course with guidance and consideration for personal situations and limitations.

269175.explanation: The CDA wants to actively support people who are distant from the labor market. Subsidized jobs provide work rhythm and perspective. Caparis plays an important role here as an expert. Together with entrepreneurs and education, we work on the transition to regular employment.

269176.explanation: The CDA believes that debt relief should remain targeted at those who truly need it. We prefer to focus on tailored solutions and debt assistance to help people move forward sustainably. This way, support remains fair, affordable, and aimed at participation.

269177.explanation: The CDA values retraining because it is specifically intended to get people back to work and actively participate in society. Job application requirements remain a national task, but the municipality supports where necessary to encourage this.

269178.explanation: The CDA considers informal care very important. At the same time, we do not want to create inequality between employed people and those receiving social assistance benefits. Arrangements and customization exist for leave and support, in collective labor agreements and care leave, so that work and care can be combined.

269179.explanation: The CDA values good accessibility and livability. We want parking spaces in the Drachten neighborhoods to be preserved so that residents and visitors can safely and easily park their cars.

269181.explanation: The CDA values accessibility, but free parking at shopping centers is not always feasible. We prefer to focus on a good balance between accessibility, livability, and affordable amenities for residents and entrepreneurs.

269182.explanation: The CDA values good accessibility. We support tailored transport between the centers and want to actively carry out research into a city bus between neighborhoods, so that residents can travel easily and safely.

269183.explanation: The CDA considers a lively and well-accessible center important. We are cautious about more car-free streets because accessibility by car remains essential for visitors, shops, and entrepreneurs in Drachten.

269184.explanation: The CDA values traffic safety, but a general 30 km/h speed limit within built-up areas is not always desirable. We opt for smart, location-specific speed restrictions where safety and traffic flow remain balanced.

269185.explanation: The CDA finds good healthcare important. People should be able to choose a healthcare provider that suits their religion, culture, or beliefs as much as possible, so that care remains personal, respectful, and appropriate for everyone in Smallingerland.

269186.explanation: Meeting places are the foundation of society. The CDA therefore values social and cultural meeting places highly, especially for youth. Every neighborhood should have a place where people can come together.

269187.explanation: The CDA believes that in youth care, quality should take precedence over price. When selecting organizations, providing good, appropriate, and reliable care for young people in Smallingerland is always paramount.

269188.explanation: The CDA believes that Smallingerland can accommodate more asylum seekers. Good coordination with the national government and the local environment is important. This way, solidarity and support go hand in hand.

269189.explanation: The CDA values safety and livability. We support strict enforcement regarding disturbances around shelter locations so that both residents nearby and those living at the locations feel safe and comfortable.

269190.explanation: The CDA values integration; language lessons and guidance are national tasks. The municipality supports where possible so that asylum seekers can participate independently in society more quickly.

269191.explanation: The CDA believes residents should be well informed. Letters, online messages, and forms can be made available in Frisian and Dutch where possible so that everyone understands the information and can participate in society.

269192.explanation: The CDA values village and neighborhood festivals for community bonding. We are positive about lower permit fees so that residents and associations can more easily organize activities together and strengthen their community.

269193.explanation: The CDA considers sports for children important. We commit to ensuring every child can play sports at minimal cost so that everyone can participate, develop talents, and grow up healthy and active in Smallingerland.

269194.explanation: The CDA believes sports are important for everyone. We oppose stopping support for people with low incomes because sports contribute to health, development, and social engagement in Smallingerland.

269195.explanation: The CDA values good education and employment. We focus on more internship and work-learning places at local companies so that vocational students gain practical experience and start stronger in the labor market in Smallingerland.

269196.explanation: The CDA values both nature and agriculture. We are cautious about expanding nature at the expense of farmland and seek solutions that strengthen nature without unnecessarily disadvantaging farmers and their income.

269197.explanation: The CDA values involved residents. For village councils and neighborhood platforms, we will double their own budgets so that residents can make their neighborhoods more beautiful and pleasant and take initiatives themselves for a livable and connected municipality.

269198.explanation: The CDA believes it is important that young people feel safe and involved. Youth will have their own meeting place in every neighborhood or village so they can meet, engage in activities, and develop in Smallingerland.

269200.explanation: The CDA believes that everyone must keep their agreements.

269201.explanation: The CDA values financial responsibility. The municipality should not simply take over debts but helps residents where needed with guidance, advice, and tailored solutions to tackle debts themselves and prevent additional problems.

269202.explanation: The CDA considers good housing important. We aim for about 7,000 additional homes, for example in Drachten-South and through infill in Drachten and villages, so there are enough homes for young and old and Smallingerland remains livable.

269204.explanation: The CDA values good accessibility. Smallingerland will continue to lobby for the Lelylijn between Groningen and Lelystad because a rail connection offers opportunities for employment, housing, and development in Smallingerland and the region.

269207.explanation: The CDA values meeting places for youth. We invest more in spaces in every neighborhood or village so that young people can meet, develop, and feel safe and involved in Smallingerland.

269208.explanation: The CDA values safety. Camera surveillance should be purposeful and temporary so that it effectively combats nuisance and crime, while residents retain their privacy and supervision remains responsible in Smallingerland.

269209.explanation: The CDA values financial responsibility. A fair distribution of burdens is paramount: no unnecessary tax increases. Raising the property tax (OZB) is not the solution to balance the budget.

269210.explanation: The CDA values a lively center. We are positive about expanding terraces in Drachten so that entrepreneurs can grow, the center remains attractive, and residents and visitors can enjoy each other's company.

269211.explanation: The CDA values financial security. We support setting multi-year subsidies so that associations, cultural institutions, and projects can plan better and develop sustainably in Smallingerland.

269212.explanation: The CDA values an accessible municipality. We invest in faster processing of permit applications so that residents and entrepreneurs are helped more efficiently and can execute their projects smoothly in Smallingerland.

269158.explanation: It is a complicated levy with relatively much hassle and little sense of fairness. We prefer simple rules and a fair distribution of burdens so that the municipality remains service-oriented and residents are not unnecessarily burdened.

269159.explanation: The municipality has a legal task to adequately house status holders. We want a fair system based on urgency and vulnerability and more affordable homes so that groups are not set against each other.

269160.explanation: Integration is not an "extra", it prevents problems and helps people participate faster: language, work, school, and meeting others. This makes society safer, closer, and fairer. This fits our approach: humane and future-oriented, with clear expectations and real opportunities.

269162.explanation: Accessibility is a basic condition for participation. Entrepreneurs themselves are responsible: it is truly hospitable only when everyone can enter. Municipal funds should primarily go to public facilities; the investment in a building lies with the entrepreneur.

269164.explanation: Sports clubs are more than sports: they mean meeting, health, and prevention. If we want young people to participate and neighborhoods to become stronger, we must help clubs to stay afloat.

269166.explanation: Safety starts with accountability. A community police officer who knows residents detects issues earlier and builds trust. This fits our commitment to a visible, locally rooted police force.

269167.explanation: Camera surveillance can be used, but only if other means are insufficient. It can help reduce violence and nuisance and track down perpetrators. Always carefully: use targeted, evaluate, and combine with prevention, youth work, and visible enforcement.

269168.explanation: Bicycle safety and respect in public spaces are non-negotiable. Enforcing rules where things go wrong protects the large group who do cycle responsibly-and prevents accidents involving children and the elderly.

269170.explanation: We do want to vigorously promote affordable housing, but not with a rigid percentage that could backfire locally. Our program opts for a healthy mix (with a minimum of social rental housing) so that first-time buyers, families, and seniors can all find truly suitable homes.

269173.explanation: Too many people now just miss out on schemes and end up in trouble as a result. We want help that truly helps: prevention is more humane and cheaper than fixing problems afterward.

269174.explanation: Participation is good, both for yourself and society. But always appropriately and with dignity, with guidance and room for health and informal care. No punishment, but perspective.

269175.explanation: For some people, a regular job is just one step too far. Subsidized jobs provide routine, self-worth, and work experience, preventing problems from piling up. That is investing in people.

269176.explanation: This affects precisely those who have to scrutinize every euro. We want to prevent debt and stress; a reliable government does not push vulnerable residents deeper into problems.

269177.explanation: Retraining is often the fastest route to sustainable employment. Give people space to learn, then you prevent revolving-door policies and help both employers and job seekers.

269178.explanation: Informal caregiving is already work, hard work. We want to support and appreciate caregivers, not add extra pressure. That way, care and community life remain intact.

269179.explanation: "Keep everything" does not help neighborhoods move forward. We want livable neighborhoods with less parking pressure on the streets, more greenery, and safe routes. Parking remains necessary but should be integrated smarter and tidier.

269181.explanation: Nothing is truly free: the costs then end up elsewhere. We choose a smart balance: attractive and accessible downtowns and shopping areas, but also fairly financed and with space for cycling and public transport.

269182.explanation: Accessibility means participation. Tailored transport helps the elderly, youth, and people without cars get to school, work, healthcare, and social activities, especially in villages.

269183.explanation: An attractive center is safe, green, and pleasant for walking and cycling. More car-free or low-traffic areas create space for terraces, socializing, and shops without losing accessibility.

269184.explanation: 30 km/h can be sensible in many places, but as a general rule everywhere it is too blunt. We choose a tailored approach: 30 where it makes residential streets safer, and sensible traffic policies on main roads.

269185.explanation: Care truly works only when someone feels seen and safe. That is why we want identity-based care to remain available when residents choose it; that is respect for human dignity.

269186.explanation: Social interaction is important, but a community center everywhere is not always necessary or affordable. Sometimes good facilities already exist. We choose tailored investment: invest where there is demonstrable need and strengthen existing meeting spots.

269187.explanation: With children, quality comes before cost-cutting. Poor help is ultimately more expensive for the child, the family, and society. We want effective help with fewer waiting lists and better collaboration around the child.

269188.explanation: We advocate for humane and future-proof asylum policies with fair distribution. Sometimes this means taking responsibility, even when it is difficult, provided reception is well organized, small-scale where possible, and has community support.

269189.explanation: Being hospitable also means setting boundaries. Nuisance undermines support and safety, so you address it quickly and decisively, with enforcement and proper guidance, so it remains workable for residents and reception locations.

269190.explanation: The sooner someone learns the language and finds their way, the faster calm and perspective arise, also for the surroundings. Integration requires effort from newcomers and a welcoming, clear society.

269191.explanation: Clear communication is a basic requirement of a service-oriented government. In Fryslân, it makes sense to serve residents in both languages, especially for important information and forms.

269192.explanation: Village and neighborhood festivals build connection. Do not make volunteer initiatives unnecessarily expensive or complicated; that is exactly what a reliable municipality should do.

269193.explanation: Sport means health, friendship, and resilience. That is why we want every child to participate, regardless of financial means, as concrete as possible: free membership of one club.

269194.explanation: This is exactly the wrong cutback. People who play sports are healthier and stronger in life. Especially for families with stress and money worries, this is an investment that prevents problems.

269195.explanation: MBO graduates are the skilled professionals of tomorrow. By connecting businesses and education, we keep talent in the region and give young people perspectives. That is good for them and for our local economy.

269196.explanation: Stewardship requires that we protect biodiversity and landscape, even if that means making choices. Always fairly: in dialogue with farmers, offering perspectives, compensation where necessary, and focusing on what has the greatest local impact.

269197.explanation: Villages and neighborhoods often know best what is needed. By granting more space to local plans, we increase ownership and create solutions that fit the place.

269198.explanation: Young people belong. Good, safe meeting places prevent nuisance and loneliness and give youth room to develop themselves, with youth work nearby and targeted enforcement where needed.

269200.explanation: Reducing support usually makes problems bigger: more stress, more debt, further from work. We choose tailored approaches and guidance: be clear about agreements, but first help so that people can actually comply.

269201.explanation: You do not solve debts structurally by routinely taking them over. We focus on early detection, strong debt assistance, and tackling profit models linked to debt. Debt takeover can be done as tailored help in special cases.

269202.explanation: We must build, absolutely, but “big” and “one new neighborhood” are not automatically the best solutions. We choose a smart mix: infill where possible, fitting growth where needed, and building in villages and Drachten while preserving greenery and good facilities.

269204.explanation: Good accessibility is crucial for work, education, and broad prosperity. We will continue to advocate for better public transport connections, including the Lelylijn, because it strengthens and future-proofs Northern Netherlands.

269207.explanation: This is investing in prevention. Young people who have a place to play sports, hang out, or organize activities build their talents and networks, which makes neighborhoods calmer and stronger.

269208.explanation: Camera surveillance is intrusive. If it demonstrably improves safety, we should be able to use it longer. Start with a pilot with clear goals and rigorous evaluation. Privacy, transparency, and periodic review always remain paramount.

269209.explanation: We pursue a cautious financial policy and do not want tax increases that affect residents and entrepreneurs. First, choose sharply, organize smartly, and prevent waste so costs remain affordable.

269210.explanation: A lively center requires space for socializing and hospitality. Terraces can help, as long as it remains safe and accessible and fits the movement toward an attractive, low-traffic center.

269211.explanation: Associations and social organizations need certainty to retain volunteers and make plans. Multi-year subsidies provide stability and continuity with clear agreements about goals and accountability.

269212.explanation: A service-oriented government means: no unnecessary delays and no wall of rules. Faster, clearer procedures help citizen initiatives and entrepreneurs, making the municipality more reliable.

269158.explanation: With the dog tax, we cover the costs of dog walking areas, plastic bags, and partly the animal ambulance and shelter. The first dog costs €79/year and the second €113/year. This discourages having many dogs. Exemptions must be allowed for assistance dogs.

269159.explanation: The municipality must ensure there are enough homes for everyone. The current shortage of (social rental) housing is not a natural phenomenon or a lack of money; it is the result of political choices.

269160.explanation: All people on the sidelines must be helped. This does not always have to be with money. The person must be central. Financial problems must be prevented because they ultimately hinder integration. Municipalities already receive money from the national government for integration support.

269162.explanation: Accessibility is important, but entrepreneurs also have their own responsibility in this. The municipality can support by providing information and raising awareness. If the municipality has to finance all adjustments, the budget is quickly depleted, leaving less for other facilities.

269164.explanation: (Financial) help from the municipality should always be considered, but the reason why an association is in financial trouble must be examined. Participating in society and doing more sports/exercise is a good way to stay healthy; we should encourage this as a municipality.

269166.explanation: We believe that first (many) more police officers must be deployed for this. More police presence on the streets can also be achieved by reducing the administrative burden on officers, giving them more time for their operational tasks on the streets.

269167.explanation: The SP is critical of camera surveillance in public spaces because its added value for safety is often unconvincing. SP Smallingerland wants more police on the streets and a district officer back in every neighborhood. Camera surveillance should always be temporary as it addresses only symptoms.

269168.explanation: The SP acknowledges that 'fatbikers' bring a 'new' form of nuisance. When this occurs, the police should intervene quickly. However, marking this entire group as suspicious is stigmatizing and unhelpful. We see a large role for street corner work in addressing these youths.

269170.explanation: There is a shortage of social rental homes in Smallingerland, so more must be built. Also, fewer social rental homes from housing corporations should be sold. The targets for corporations must be increased and they should be facilitated in implementation.

269173.explanation: The municipality has unjustly lowered this income threshold in recent years, causing problems for many residents. Due to high energy, housing, and food prices (caused by excessive profits!), more people are getting into trouble.

269174.explanation: People cannot find paid work, but suddenly these jobs are available when on social assistance in the form of obligatory community services? Fair pay for fair work! Our municipality always has the responsibility to guide people with distance to the labor market to meaningful work!

269175.explanation: As long as necessary, we must simply do it. Fair pay for fair work. Much is already being done, but some social functions add more value to our society than they generate financially. The government must help here.

269176.explanation: Of course, we want everyone to participate and nobody to fall through the cracks. When people incur debts or are excluded, problems worsen. Therefore, debt relief remains necessary for residents with low incomes.

269177.explanation: Naturally dependent on the time requirements of the study.

269178.explanation: No one should be in a situation where they have to apply for a job, get hired, but then cannot come to work because they need to provide informal care.

269179.explanation: The livability and harmony in neighborhoods must not deteriorate. Redesigning streets and suddenly having far fewer parking spaces is not efficient.

269181.explanation: Most people already struggle to pay for groceries. Introducing paid parking suddenly only costs people money without providing them any benefit.

269182.explanation: Only at locations where necessary. We also see a role for neighborhood cars, such as in the Wiken (order one day in advance). A fine-meshed network is pleasant for those in need and will ultimately increase usage.

269183.explanation: The pedestrian area in the center is large enough. However, traffic flows should improve, one-way traffic introduced, and more safe intersections and roundabouts created.

269184.explanation: In principle, this is a good idea. At 30 km/h, there are far fewer severe or fatal accidents. The time difference is minimal. Also, cars emit fewer harmful gases. However, within Drachten, some access and exit roads are well designed for 50 km/h.

269185.explanation: Care is always tailored; if possible yes, but we cannot currently impose this on healthcare. There is already a severe shortage of care workers and turnover is high. This would only add more stress to care. The core task is to provide care.

269186.explanation: The neighborhood is the scale of the future. Meeting places must be established in every district or village. A place where youth can enter and where small gatherings and clubs can meet easily. Social organizations can make arrangements here.

269187.explanation: This only means that organizations must cut budgets, and so many cuts have already been made during decentralization. Select based on price/quality ratio; let results weigh heavily.

269188.explanation: We agree with this, but municipalities that accommodate none or few asylum seekers must also be required to provide reception. National coordination is needed here.

269189.explanation: But do we have much trouble with this here? And why should additional effort only be made at reception locations for nuisance? We support more police on the streets, and enforcement must always be swift where (temporary) nuisance occurs.

269190.explanation: Isn't this the norm now? For status holders to participate well, practical guidance and speaking our language are a must.

269191.explanation: Our municipality promotes in many ways that Frisian must be supported and preserved. It is only fair that they themselves adhere to this. However, if it appears that certain messages or documents in Frisian are not consulted, tailored solutions must naturally be provided.

269192.explanation: Village and neighborhood parties create encounters, cohesion, and livability in our villages and neighborhoods. They are organized by volunteers with limited resources. The SP believes the municipality should encourage this instead of taxing it. This can be done by keeping permit costs low.

269193.explanation: Sports are healthy and important to learn at a young age. Sports prevent health problems later in life and are therefore a good preventive method to keep healthcare costs low. Healthy people live longer, and this must not only be for the wealthy.

269194.explanation: Sports are healthy and important to learn at a young age. Sports prevent health problems later in life and are therefore a good preventive method to keep healthcare costs low. Healthy people live longer, and this must not only be for the wealthy.

269195.explanation: The municipality already does a lot between the 3 O’s (Government, Businesses, Education), but we still receive signals that education does not align well with business needs. Good communication about this and encouraging proactive developments is certainly a role for our municipality.

269196.explanation: The situation is very bad for meadow birds and insects in the Netherlands. Bees are also struggling. Our municipality must endeavor to reverse this; this can be done in consultation and does not need to threaten farmers but can actually be an opportunity.

269197.explanation: If there are sufficient (good) initiatives in the neighborhood, our municipality should simply facilitate them. A ceiling decided behind a desk need not mean a project cannot proceed. As far as the SP knows, neighborhood budgets are not always fully used.

269198.explanation: The neighborhood is the scale of the future. Every neighborhood or village must have a meeting place for youth, associations, and social activities.

269200.explanation: The SP opposes reducing social assistance benefits as a sanction when someone fails to meet work or education agreements. Social assistance is not a favor but a safety net to live on. Reducing it pushes people further into poverty and insecurity. Guidance is better!

269201.explanation: The municipality must prevent people from falling into debt through early detection. This is always tailored; the municipality could first arrange a settlement with creditors, who would then receive partial repayment, ultimately saving costs for both municipality and creditors.

269202.explanation: The housing shortage is now so severe that small projects no longer help. Building a complete neighborhood offers advantages. There can be a clear vision to ensure excellent livability in the new neighborhood, including the facilities a self-sufficient neighborhood needs.

269204.explanation: The SP supports more public transport. Around the Lelylijn, there are stories of enormous economic profits, 100,000 homes to be built, and other fairy tales. We do not want prestige but a railway line for people that connects to Europe and reduces flying within Europe.

269207.explanation: Meeting spaces for young people are important for relaxation, development, and prevention. The municipality already does a lot, but opening hours do not always match when young people are free. More investment means better coordination and more safe places for youth.

269208.explanation: The SP is critical of camera surveillance in public spaces because its added value for safety is often unconvincing. SP Smallingerland wants more police on the streets and a district officer back in every neighborhood. Camera surveillance should always be temporary as it addresses only symptoms.

269209.explanation: The SP currently sees no reason to increase property tax (OZB). Due to the sharply risen house prices, the municipality already receives more revenue. Increasing OZB to generate extra income arbitrarily is wrong. However, an increase is not taboo for us!

269210.explanation: The use of our center is changing from shopping to meeting. More hospitality and conviviality require space. Under clear conditions and where possible, terraces should be allowed to expand. This offers opportunities for a lively center and for the entertainment area De Kaden.

269211.explanation: A subsidy is given to achieve a desired result; here too, results and quality must be central. If a project lasts several years, it must be possible to establish multi-year subsidies. This also applies if investments are needed by our partners.

269212.explanation: In principle, our municipality wants to issue permits cost-neutral, meaning: the applicant pays for the administrative costs. However, in practice, processes take too long and are too expensive. This hinders developments we actually want. Faster processing offers opportunities!

269159.explanation: No matter how distressing the situation sometimes is: we believe that priority in housing allocation is not justified. Many other home seekers have also been waiting for a long time. Failing national policy should not be passed on to the local housing market and other vulnerable groups.

269160.explanation: This is a task for the national government. The municipality must make maximum use of the resources made available by the State. Municipal budgets are already under great pressure due to the extra tasks dumped on them by the national government.

269162.explanation: The municipality is obliged to make its own buildings and public facilities accessible to people with disabilities. This obligation also applies to entrepreneurs. The municipality's role is mainly to supervise and enforce this, not to take over that responsibility and costs.

269164.explanation: Sports clubs have their own financial responsibility. The municipality must ensure a fair and transparent subsidy policy for all clubs, but structural financial support for deficits is not a given.

269167.explanation: The nightlife area of Drachten is perceived as very unsafe. Camera surveillance can help to identify perpetrators, prevent incidents, and provide protection to victims.

269168.explanation: It is important to strictly enforce against antisocial behavior and nuisance. We want everyone to feel (again) safe.

269170.explanation: For us, 31% social rental housing is the minimum, but tailored solutions based on housing needs are decisive.

269173.explanation: Currently, an income limit of 115% is applied. This is a realistic and acceptable percentage.

269174.explanation: No one benefits if people are forced into social work.

269175.explanation: Subsidizing jobs will cost the municipality a lot of money. In the current overheated labor market, the long-term unemployed can be helped to find jobs through reintegration programs.

269176.explanation: Stopping the cancellation of municipal taxes will push people below the poverty line.

269177.explanation: Further training increases the chance to support oneself again, which provides peace of mind and is more important than mandatory job applications.

269178.explanation: This depends on the care needs that the informal caregiver must provide; this is a matter of tailored solutions.

269181.explanation: We want to create a lively, vibrant center and counteract vacancies. Entrepreneurs are under pressure and have to compete with surrounding villages where parking is free. With free parking, Drachten can distinguish itself from large shopping centers like Leeuwarden, Groningen, and Sneek.

269183.explanation: A car-free center contradicts the desire for a lively and vibrant center and will discourage even more people from shopping in Drachten.

269184.explanation: For residential neighborhoods this is a fine existing arrangement, but for access roads to and from industrial estates and office parks it is not realistic (e.g., Noorder- and Zuiderhogeweg). This may also have consequences for public transport.

269185.explanation: The quality of the care provider is always decisive for us.

269186.explanation: This can contribute to the livability of villages and neighborhoods.

269187.explanation: Selection should mainly be based on quality, reliability, and integrity. Cheap can be expensive in the end.

269188.explanation: We wish everyone a dignified existence. At the same time, shelter must remain bearable for society. Due to the great pressure on education, care, and livability (in the neighborhoods), integration and mutual acceptance are at risk.

269189.explanation: Strict enforcement is always important in cases of nuisance, both around asylum seekers centers and nuisance caused by fatbikers or drugs. Clear rules and consistent enforcement ensure safety, livability, and support in society.

269190.explanation: It is important for both newcomers and the local community to integrate quickly into society.

269191.explanation: We are proud of our Frisian language and culture, but research shows that most Frisians have difficulty reading and especially writing Frisian.

269192.explanation: Events are crucial for social cohesion. The municipality should therefore simplify administrative procedures so that volunteers and entrepreneurs are relieved and supported.

269193.explanation: We have a solid poverty policy. Within current regulations, budgets are already available for children who cannot participate in sports for financial reasons.

269194.explanation: Sport is important for health, also for people with limited funds.

269195.explanation: The existing cooperation between government, entrepreneurs, and education is important, but everyone has their own role.

269196.explanation: The municipality acting alone can conflict with the policies of surrounding municipalities, the province, and the state. Cooperation is important for support and success.

269197.explanation: Neighborhood and village budgets are currently arranged in municipal policy and are sufficient.

269198.explanation: Young people deserve attention and their own meeting place; this can prevent nuisance and problems.

269200.explanation: The social assistance benefit is a safety net, and it is reasonable to expect people to exit it as soon as possible. This requires cooperation with the municipality towards financial independence, with clear mutual agreements.

269201.explanation: People also have their own responsibility. The municipality offers support when necessary.

269202.explanation: Drachten, as a large core, must take responsibility for large-scale housing construction. This preserves green areas and prevents fragmented building that puts pressure on surrounding villages. This is independent of the possible arrival of the Lelylijn.

269204.explanation: This is important for public transport accessibility and the economic development of Smallingerland. Whether this is via the Lelylijn or another rail solution, we keep open; a rail connection is very important.

269207.explanation: Young people deserve attention and their own meeting place; this can prevent nuisance and problems.

269208.explanation: Where safety is under pressure, the municipality must be able to deploy permanent supervision. This effectively tackles nuisance and crime and keeps the public space safe for residents.

269209.explanation: Residents are not cash cows. Higher charges especially affect people with limited income. The municipality must first budget efficiently and prevent waste before resorting to tax increases.

269210.explanation: Expanding terraces strengthens the local economy, creates a pleasant atmosphere, and attracts more visitors. It makes the center livelier and more attractive.

269211.explanation: Larger organizations need to know what to expect. That provides stability, prevents uncertainty, and ensures they can invest in activities that the municipality values long-term. Different rules apply for one-off subsidies.

269212.explanation: This prevents delays for residents and entrepreneurs. It reduces costs, increases predictability, and makes projects executable faster. Investing in capacity and simplicity pays off because the municipality thus improves service and trust.

269158.explanation: The dog tax yields few facilities, while the inspections affect privacy and the implementation is costly. This is disproportionate. General facilities should be paid from general funds.

269159.explanation: The housing shortage is severe and waiting times are long. Priority for status holders feels unfair to residents who have been waiting for years. Everyone deserves equal opportunities; the solution lies in more affordable housing, not in favoring one group.

269160.explanation: Integration is important, but more money is not automatically the solution. It is about an efficient use of resources. The municipality must focus on language and work, without compromising its care tasks.

269162.explanation: Accessibility is essential, but entrepreneurs also have their own responsibility in this. The municipality itself faces major challenges to make public spaces and municipal buildings accessible. This should be the priority.

269164.explanation: Sports clubs play an important social role. They provide opportunities for social interaction, health, and engagement. Due to higher municipal charges, some clubs are under pressure. Targeted support helps preserve this social foundation.

269166.explanation: Contact between residents and the neighborhood police officer is important, but a fixed weekly consultation hour in every neighborhood and village is not realistic. Police deployment must remain flexible and aligned with where needs and problems are greatest.

269167.explanation: Camera surveillance can contribute to safety in the nightlife area of Drachten. It works preventively and helps address incidents. It should be deployed in a targeted and temporary manner, with clear rules and respect for privacy.

269168.explanation: Anti-social behavior must be addressed, but extra inspections focused on one specific group are undesirable. Enforcement should focus on behavior, not on the type of transport used.

269170.explanation: Social housing is important, but a fixed percentage is too rigid. With already 31% social housing, a balance is needed. The municipality should manage a varied housing supply that meets local demand so that middle incomes and starters also have housing prospects.

269173.explanation: More and more people just manage to get by, even when working. The current income threshold does not always reflect reality. By raising the threshold, the municipality can prevent people from getting into unnecessary trouble.

269174.explanation: Socially valuable work can contribute to structure, self-confidence, and participation in society. Extra hands are especially welcome now. This must be appropriate, voluntary where possible, and not displace paid work. Customization and humanity are paramount.

269175.explanation: Work is important, but creating a lot of extra subsidized jobs is not automatically the solution. The risk of displacement looms. The focus should be on guidance, development, and transition to regular jobs, with customization per person.

269176.explanation: Remission of debts should not be a standard arrangement. The municipality must focus on self-reliance and customization. In exceptional situations, remission may still be necessary but always as a temporary solution and part of guidance towards perspective.

269177.explanation: Further training increases the chance of sustainable employment. It is logical that people can temporarily fully focus on this. This requires customization and clear agreements to ensure training actually leads to better opportunities in the labor market.

269178.explanation: Informal care is of great value to society and often demands much time and effort. It is reasonable that informal caregivers are temporarily exempt from the obligation to apply for jobs. This requires customization and good coordination to prevent overload.

269179.explanation: Parking spaces are important for livability in the neighborhoods. Many residents depend on the car, also due to work, care, or informal care. Removing parking spaces increases pressure and nuisance in residential areas.

269181.explanation: Free parking at shopping centers lowers the threshold to shop locally. It supports entrepreneurs and ensures that residents and visitors can easily reach the shops, even if they depend on a car.

269182.explanation: Accessibility is important, but structural transport between centers is not a core task of the municipality. Public transport and tailored transport primarily belong to the province and transport providers. The municipality should identify bottlenecks and seek cooperation, not become an operator itself.

269183.explanation: A lively center requires good accessibility for everyone. More car-free streets limit accessibility for elderly people, less mobile residents, and entrepreneurs. Customization is needed, not further extensive closure of the center of Drachten.

269184.explanation: Traffic safety is important, but a general 30 km/h speed limit is poorly enforceable and unrealistic. There is a big difference between residential streets and arterial roads. Without support and enforcement, the rule loses credibility.

269185.explanation: Good and accessible care is paramount. Due to staff shortages and limited capacity, it is not always possible to meet all personal preferences.

269186.explanation: Meeting and cohesion are important, but a community center in every neighborhood or village is not realistic. Needs differ per area. The municipality should listen to residents and focus on customization and good use of existing facilities.

269187.explanation: Quality must come first in youth care. We know the cheapest provider is not automatically the best. When choosing organizations, the municipality should consider expertise, reliability, and what they have achieved in practice.

269188.explanation: We want to be honest about shelter. Decisions must be supported by residents. The capacity of the residents is leading. Doing more than required therefore does not seem desirable or necessary.

269189.explanation: Local residents accommodate these people and thereby show solidarity. For this reason, their livability and safety must be guaranteed. Nuisance is unacceptable and requires clear and consistent enforcement to maintain support.

269190.explanation: Asylum seekers with a realistic chance of residence often already receive language lessons, including via the COA. Practical guidance is already applied where needed. Early focus on language and guidance increases self-reliance and benefits both newcomers and the Smallingerland community.

269191.explanation: Frisian is our recognized language and part of the identity of Smallingerland. Residents should be able to receive municipal information in Frisian or Dutch. This requires a practical and efficient approach, fitting the municipality’s capabilities.

269192.explanation: Village and neighborhood festivals strengthen the community. They are often organized by volunteers. High permit costs are a barrier. Reduction helps keep these initiatives possible and accessible in Smallingerland. They form the cement of our society!

269193.explanation: Sports must be accessible to every child. The municipality should help make at least one sport affordable. Solidarity is important, but not unlimited. This keeps support fair and targeted at equal opportunities.

269194.explanation: For adults, personal responsibility applies. Sport is important, but it is not a primary municipal task to finance. Municipal support should focus on children and vulnerable groups, not on structurally paying for adult sports.

269195.explanation: Internship and work-learning places are crucial for vocational students to gain practical experience. By cooperating with local businesses, the municipality increases work opportunities, retains talent in the region, and strengthens Smallingerland’s local economy.

269196.explanation: Nature is important, but the number of farmers is declining quickly. Expanding nature should not automatically lead to the loss of even more agricultural land. The municipality must ensure balance and offer farmers perspective.

269197.explanation: Neighborhood and village budgets strengthen involvement and customization. However, higher budgets should not serve as compensation for the rising costs faced by village halls. Preventing financial distress is better than curing it!

269198.explanation: Meeting places for young people are important, but a fixed place in every neighborhood or village is not always necessary or desirable. Needs differ per area. The municipality should listen to youth and residents and focus on customization.

269200.explanation: Agreements about work or education are there for a reason. Those who do not comply without a valid reason may be addressed accordingly. Reducing benefits can then be appropriate, with attention to customization and personal circumstances.

269201.explanation: Taking over debts may seem a quick solution but does not solve the cause and can increase dependency. This already happens only in very exceptional cases and under strict conditions. That must remain so. The municipality should primarily focus on guidance, debt assistance, and prevention.

269202.explanation: That is not currently at issue. The housing shortage requires building, but one large new residential area is not necessarily the best solution. The municipality must listen to residents and build according to needs, allowing room for customization in and around Drachten.

269204.explanation: The Lelylijn is very important for the North Netherlands, but decision-making lies with the national government and province. Smallingerland can continue to emphasize its importance, while focusing its efforts on feasible improvements in accessibility that benefit residents now.

269207.explanation: Meeting spaces for young people can be valuable, but more funds are not always the solution. Needs differ by neighborhood and village. The municipality should invest where it is really needed and where there is support.

269208.explanation: Camera surveillance can help address nuisance and insecurity, but must not become a permanent tool. It should be temporary, targeted, and regularly evaluated, with respect for privacy and proportionality.

269209.explanation: The burdens on residents and entrepreneurs are already high enough. The municipality must first critically examine its own expenditures and priorities before raising taxes. The basics must be in order first! Raising the property tax (ozb) is not the right path.

269210.explanation: Terraces contribute to a lively city center. Expansion should be possible, with attention to safety, accessibility, and without causing nuisance.

269211.explanation: Multi-year subsidies provide clarity and stability for activities such as sports clubs and village events. At the same time, the municipality must keep room for incidental one-year subsidies. Not every activity or investment requires a multi-year arrangement.

269212.explanation: No one wants long waiting times. Whether you want to build, renovate, or organize an event: you want quick clarity. The municipality must ensure that permits are processed smoothly and clearly.

269158.explanation: The dog tax generates structural income that is used for facilities and maintenance in public spaces.

269159.explanation: The municipality must focus on more building and faster turnover, so that no one is given preferential treatment and the pressure decreases.

269160.explanation: Integration is mainly about learning the language, finding work, and participating - this requires good implementation, not necessarily more money.

269162.explanation: The VVD believes that building accessibility is important for an inclusive society. Where necessary, the municipality can support entrepreneurs to remove barriers for people with disabilities. This must be done in a targeted and efficient manner, complementary to existing national schemes and tax options. Municipal aid can be especially useful for local initiatives or adaptations that otherwise would not get off the ground.

269164.explanation: But always under clear conditions: transparency, personal responsibility, and a future plan.

269166.explanation: But in consultation with the police: organize practically and feasibly, with room for customization where needed.

269167.explanation: But under clear conditions: careful deployment, privacy protection, and regular evaluation for effectiveness.

269168.explanation: It’s not about the type of bike, but about behavior; enforcement must focus on that.

269170.explanation: It’s about a guideline percentage, not a dogma: application requires customization per location and periodic recalibration based on current figures (which may fluctuate).

269173.explanation: The focus should be on participation, work, and self-reliance, so that help is temporary and offers perspective.

269174.explanation: Always with customization: taking into account health, informal care, and personal circumstances.

269175.explanation: Public money must be used for perspective and independence, not to maintain temporary arrangements.

269176.explanation: For people with low incomes, municipal taxes can make the difference between making ends meet and falling into debt.

269177.explanation: Always temporary and with clear agreements: complete the training = quicker entry into work.

269178.explanation: The VVD believes that people who provide intensive informal care should not be unnecessarily burdened with job application requirements. If someone demonstrably spends a lot of time caring for a family member or acquaintance, the municipality should have the scope to grant temporary exemption. This must always be based on customization and individual assessment, so work and care can be combined responsibly.

269179.explanation: Changes can only happen if there are realistic alternatives and in consultation with residents.

269181.explanation: Parking policy should support the economic functioning of shopping areas, not hinder it.

269182.explanation: But organize in a targeted and smart way, in collaboration with existing initiatives and with attention to costs.

269183.explanation: Livability and safety can also be improved with smart design and enforcement, without further excluding cars.

269184.explanation: Traffic safety requires customization per street or neighborhood, not one standard for everything.

269185.explanation: This does not mean the municipality must organize or pay for everything; it is up to the care provider to arrange this.

269186.explanation: The municipality must facilitate where initiatives arise, but not take on all real estate and operations everywhere.

269187.explanation: The VVD wants to make a Bibob test mandatory during selection to prevent fraud, unscrupulous providers, and misuse of care funds.

269188.explanation: VVD Smallingerland stands for realistic and manageable shelter, especially for groups at risk. For Safe Country residents, clear limits are necessary.

269189.explanation: Nuisance is unacceptable, regardless of who causes it.

269190.explanation: It reduces the chance of nuisance, isolation, and long-term dependency.

269191.explanation: Targeted use of Frisian is fine where it adds value, but not as a standard for everything.

269192.explanation: Village and neighborhood parties are important for livability and social cohesion.

269193.explanation: Through targeted schemes, the municipality can deliver customization without high structural costs.

269194.explanation: Always stay alert to efficiency and abuse: help for those who really need it, with clear conditions.

269195.explanation: This responsibility primarily lies with the schools in cooperation with companies. The municipality can mainly connect and facilitate, and cannot impose anything.

269196.explanation: Nature and agriculture must be developed in balance, preferably through cooperation and voluntariness.

269197.explanation: Small amounts can trigger great involvement and own initiative.

269198.explanation: Fill in practically: simple, fitting the neighborhood and in consultation with residents, no expensive facilities or one-size-fits-all.

269200.explanation: Always with customization: first conversation and guidance, sanctions only as a last resort.

269201.explanation: Taking over is only justifiable in exceptional situations; it must not become a standard instrument.

269202.explanation: It offers space for a good mix of social rental, mid-range rental, and ownership.

269204.explanation: VVD Smallingerland chooses to look ahead and seize opportunities, even if realization is not tomorrow - lobbying is needed right now.

269207.explanation: Young people need safe places to meet each other.

269208.explanation: Extension is only possible in case of persistent, demonstrable problems, but always with a new decision and clear justification.

269209.explanation: VVD Smallingerland opts for firm choices and working more efficiently, not for shifting deficits onto residents.

269210.explanation: Yes, but with clear ground rules: accessibility, safety, and consideration of local residents must be taken into account.

269211.explanation: Yes, but always under conditions: clear objectives, interim evaluation, and the possibility to adjust.

269212.explanation: Yes, with a focus on efficiency and digitalization, not just additional rules or layers.

269158.explanation: D66 understands the idea behind the dog tax, but we see too little of the intended effect. Therefore, we support abolishing it. However, we do believe a new system should replace it, as maintenance must continue.

269159.explanation: D66 believes everyone should be treated equally. The oldest part of Article One of our constitution states that all members of society have the right to the same benefits. Therefore, in social housing, everyone should have equal opportunities.

269160.explanation: Integration from day one is a priority for D66. Investing in this is crucial for our entire society. For example, better language lessons broaden support among our residents and offer asylum seekers better opportunities.

269162.explanation: Everyone should be able to participate in our society, including people with disabilities. But it can be difficult for companies to adapt their premises accordingly; therefore, D66 supports the municipality assisting companies in these cases, for example through a loan system.

269164.explanation: A sports club is a place where everyone in society comes together. In our digital era, this happens less often. That is why we must fully support sports clubs. If they are struggling, we must support them in every possible way.

269166.explanation: D66 supports this idea, provided there is a genuine need from the neighborhoods themselves. We will not unnecessarily burden our police. If there is a real need, we should certainly discuss with the police to organize such meetings.

269167.explanation: Camera surveillance helps improve the feeling of safety and assists the police in tracking individuals who commit theft or use violence against others. However, the municipality must collaborate closely with police and enforcement officers to facilitate monitoring these images.

269168.explanation: D66 supports generally tougher enforcement of rude behavior in traffic. This does not need to target a specific group; it should apply to all forms of unsafe and rude behavior in traffic in our view.

269170.explanation: D66 considers social housing very important, which is why we gladly support the existing frameworks, including a 30% social housing threshold. There should never be less than that in Smallingerland.

269173.explanation: The municipality must keep its doors open for every resident, especially for advisory help; there should be no income threshold for this. For financial support, D66 currently wants to maintain the existing limit but is open to raising it if it becomes clear too many residents encounter problems.

269174.explanation: There are many people who rightfully use welfare benefits because they cannot work. D66 also sees that abuse occurs, but we are not open to establishing general rules that would also affect others.

269175.explanation: The municipality should always continue to support the long-term unemployed. Offering subsidized jobs should be considered on a case-by-case basis, so expanding such programs in advance is not a commitment D66 wants to make.

269176.explanation: Municipal taxes should be able to be waived in exceptional cases for residents with very low incomes. If this option is used, D66 wants the municipality to help these individuals prevent this in the future.

269177.explanation: Education can mean a fresh start for many. Therefore, D66 believes that if someone on welfare decides to pursue further education, they should temporarily be exempt from applying for jobs. This obligation naturally returns once the education ends.

269178.explanation: Informal caregiving is a full-time job and more; D66 believes it deserves much more appreciation. Therefore, the municipality should be open-minded about exempting affected residents from the job application requirement.

269179.explanation: We live in a society with many cars. Therefore, parking spaces should definitely remain in all neighborhoods. D66 does see value in more greenery in parking areas to avoid large gray areas in residential neighborhoods, for example by integrating greenery into the parking lots themselves.

269181.explanation: Parking spaces need maintenance too, especially the underground parking garage in the center. D66 does not want to increase municipal taxes for these matters and therefore prefers to keep paid parking so the user actually pays.

269182.explanation: If it is up to D66, this can be much broader than a call bus. We often hear complaints that public transport is no longer feasible due to cancellations or cuts. Therefore, we aim to provide every neighborhood and village with a bus stop served regularly daily.

269183.explanation: According to D66, all necessary streets have already been made car-free. The remaining streets in the center connect important parking lots and residential neighborhoods. It is not intended that residents can no longer use their cars; therefore, D66 does not want to make more center streets car-free.

269184.explanation: D66 prefers to keep the existing speed limits but wants to enforce them much more strictly. We want to do this through increased camera surveillance and by designing roads in school zones and residential areas to make speeding more difficult.

269185.explanation: Care is something people often need in their most vulnerable moments. Therefore, it is crucial that someone feels comfortable with the person providing their care. D66 wants to maintain the freedom for people to make this choice themselves.

269186.explanation: Culture is very important for a municipality’s character; it brings people from all sides of society together. D66 therefore thinks it is a very good idea to give every neighborhood and village its own center where they can come together.

269187.explanation: Childcare and youth care must always prioritize quality over quantity. For D66, the idea of primarily cost-saving approaches in youth care will never work.

269188.explanation: According to the distribution law, every municipality has a responsibility for a designated number of asylum seekers. Since this law exists, D66 believes the municipality should adhere to it.

269189.explanation: The safety of our residents comes first; if that means stricter supervision is needed in specific places, D66 supports that. This promotes safety for everyone, including residents of reception centers.

269190.explanation: Language lessons and other practical guidance are necessary for good integration into our society. The municipality should explore the possibility of making this mandatory for all asylum seekers to give them a better chance at a good life in the Netherlands.

269191.explanation: Friesland is a multilingual province with a rich cultural history. As a party that strongly supports culture, D66 believes the municipality should provide information both in Dutch and Frisian.

269192.explanation: According to D66, permit costs are fine as they are now, amounts less than 200 euros for small or medium-sized events. A village or neighborhood festival attracts more than enough visitors to cover these costs.

269193.explanation: Sports are a crucial part of children’s upbringing; they learn to cooperate and make friends. Income should not be a barrier according to D66. Therefore, tailored approaches should be used to ensure sports remain affordable for every family.

269194.explanation: Everyone has the right to a healthy life; this should not depend on income. Therefore, D66 believes the municipality must absolutely continue paying for sports for people in poverty.

269195.explanation: The municipality must always look for ways to offer more opportunities to our young residents. Drachten has many students from vocational (mbo), higher professional (hbo), and university (wo) education. We want the municipality to explore whether local companies are interested in offering internships and learning work placements for them all.

269196.explanation: If large plots of land become available in Smallingerland, D66 would like to develop them with sustainable housing in green neighborhoods. Therefore, we cannot commit to expanding nature and meadow bird areas. However, D66 does want to reintroduce as much nature as possible in the new housing developments.

269197.explanation: If residents come up with initiatives to improve their neighborhood or village, the municipality should have funds available that neighborhood or village interests can access. This way, we can jointly make our municipality more attractive.

269198.explanation: We hear a lot about nuisance caused by young people, but the underlying problems are never addressed. There are too few places in Smallingerland where young people can gather to have fun. If it were up to D66, investments would be made in this area.

269200.explanation: There are rules attached to the social assistance benefit; if a person does not comply, enforcement should take place. If this occurs frequently, more drastic measures should be possible.

269201.explanation: D66 believes that for such measures, the municipality should assess each time whether this is an option. Promising that the municipality will do this more often is unnecessary because it depends on how many people qualify.

269202.explanation: With a housing crisis that continues to grow, the municipality must consider the option of building a new residential area in Smallingerland. This way, we can provide a place for our own youth and attract new residents.

269204.explanation: The Lelylijn (rail line) would provide many opportunities for Smallingerland; young people can study wherever they want, the local economy grows due to a potential increase in residents, and Drachten’s accessibility improves greatly. Therefore, the lobbying for this railway must absolutely continue.

269207.explanation: We hear a lot about nuisance caused by young people, but the underlying problems are never addressed. There are too few places in Smallingerland where young people can gather to have fun. If it were up to D66, investments would be made in this area.

269208.explanation: Permanent camera surveillance is urgently needed in places like bus stations, quays, and schools to protect residents and quickly suppress criminal behavior. D66 is always in favor of strict enforcement when necessary.

269209.explanation: D66 stands for a fair, forward-looking municipality. An increase in property tax (OZB) does not consider the ability to pay. Apart from the inflation correction, we see no reason to support any further increase.

269210.explanation: If a local hospitality entrepreneur wants to expand, the municipality should allow this as much as possible if their plans fit in the public space. The municipality should cooperate as much as possible with local businesses that promote our local economy, says D66.

269211.explanation: A structural subsidy provides security and a better foundation for projects to truly achieve their objectives. Therefore, the municipality should increasingly consider multi-year subsidies.

269212.explanation: D66 believes the municipality has a responsibility towards its citizens to meet deadlines. If a small one-time investment is needed to ensure smooth operations in the future, D66 can support this.

269158.explanation: Dog tax dates back to the late Middle Ages as a measure against rabies and stray dogs. That is outdated. Moreover, currently, the revenues are not linked to costs-such as dog waste bins-incurred by the municipality related to these animals.

269159.explanation: Everyone has the right to decent housing. At the same time, there is a shortage of homes for young people and small families. Therefore, FNP supports making performance agreements that allocate a fixed portion of social rental homes to refugees with residence permits.

269160.explanation: It is important that newcomers to our municipality integrate into our society as quickly as possible. This provides more job opportunities, reduces potential social unrest, and increases mutual understanding.
